summary: School of landscape painting in early 16th-century Germany/Austria, named after the Danube River. Members include Albrecht Altdorfer and Wolf Huber. Altdorfer created one of the earliest pure landscapes in oil (Landscape with Footbridge). Known for the 'world landscape' style.

works.name: General
works.description: Early 16th-century school of landscape painting named after the Danube River, based in southern Germany and Austria. Known for the 'world landscape' style. Key members: Albrecht Altdorfer (leader), Wolf Huber, and Lucas Cranach the Elder (associated). Altdorfer's Landscape with Footbridge (1520) is one of the earliest pure landscapes in oil. The most famous painting from the school is Altdorfer's The Battle of Alexander at Issus, part of a cycle commissioned by William IV of Bavaria. Analogous to the American Hudson River School in being named after a river. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
The Danube School was an early 16th-century school of landscape painting in southern Germany and Austria, named after the Danube River. It was known for the 'world landscape' style, in which vast panoramic vistas incorporate rivers, mountains, and atmospheric effects. The school is analogous to the American Hudson River School in being named after a major river.

The leading member was Albrecht Altdorfer, who created Landscape with Footbridge (1520), considered one of the first pure landscapes in Western oil painting. The most famous painting to come out of the school is Altdorfer's The Battle of Alexander at Issus, part of a cycle commissioned by William IV of Bavaria for the Alte Pinakothek. This massive painting features a large banner suspended in the sky, a retreating crescent moon (symbolizing Muslim defeat), and an unrealistic vista showing both Cyprus and the Nile River. Altdorfer also painted several works celebrating Regensburg's expulsion of the Jews in 1519 and made copies of paintings by Lucas Cranach the Elder. His engravings influenced the Little Masters.

Wolf Huber was another significant member of the school. Altdorfer's St. George Overcoming the Dragon typifies the school's approach, with the narrative action occupying only a small portion of the canvas while a lush forest dominates.
